schools.summary: Chrysostom: the Jewish Passover (deliverance from Egypt by the lamb's blood) a shadow of ours (freedom from sin and Satan by the Son of God); Bede: 'Phase' = passing over (the angel sparing the blood-marked first-born) — Christ the true Passover sacrificed at the type's season (1 Corinthians 5:7).

themes: the Passion at the type's season

schools.summary: Robertson: the conative imperfect 'were seeking' to make away with him; Gill: met at Caiaphas's palace; Clarke: the people held Christ a prophet (the crucifixion mob got up by the priests); the rulers' fear of the people the providential restraint holding back the plot until the appointed hour, channelling it toward secret betrayal.

themes: sought how they might kill him
restrained by fear until his hour

schools.summary: Titus: Satan entered Judas not by force but 'finding the door open' (his settled covetousness) — temptation does not excuse, the open door his own greed; Chrysostom: the aggravation that he came to the mystery with the betrayal in his heart, unmoved by fear or gratitude; 'one of the twelve' — the basest treachery from the chosen circle.

debates.question: Does Satan's entrance remove Judas's responsibility?

sides.position: No — Satan entered by the 'open door' of Judas's own covetousness; the will consented

schools.summary: Gill: Judas went his way from Christ; Lightfoot: the 'captains' the Jewish temple-officers (not the Roman garrison, who had no part in the plot); Judas took the initiative, going of his own accord to negotiate the betrayal — calculated, premeditated treachery, the progress of sin from inward consent to outward conspiracy.

themes: communed with the chief priests and captains

schools.summary: Robertson: the ingressive aorist 'were glad' — exultation that one of the Twelve offered the betrayal; Luke says only 'money'; Clarke: the thirty pieces (Matthew) the price of the meanest slave (Exodus 21:32; Zechariah 11:12), fulfilling prophecy — the rulers' malicious joy, Christ valued at a slave's price, covetousness sealing the bargain.

Gill: Judas bound himself by promise to deliver Christ; Ellicott: 'in the absence of the multitude' = without a tumult; he sought opportunity, patiently watching for the moment to betray Christ apart from the crowds the rulers feared — the cold deliberation and patience of the committed traitor, his watching serving the appointed hour.

themes: in the absence of the multitude

schools.summary: Titus: Christ ate the typical Passover to remove the figure that the truth might take its place; Bede: the 14th day, the lamb slain towards evening; Eusebius: Christ did not keep it at the Jews' hour of sacrifice (they sacrificed on the Preparation, when Christ suffered) — type and antitype converging, the old feast fulfilled and abolished (1 Corinthians 5:7).

themes: the true Lamb at the type's season

schools.summary: Ellicott: Luke alone names the two as Peter and John (often together — the tomb, the temple, Acts) — a loving insight binding them at the time when Peter would soon need John's sympathy after his fall; Christ's orderly provision for the feast, committing the preparation to his chief disciples with foresight and love.

Gill: not asking in what town (the Passover fixed at Jerusalem, Deuteronomy 16:5-6) but in what house; Robertson: the deliberative 'where wilt thou that we make ready?' — the disciples' prompt, entire obedience, asking only the place, awaiting the Master's direction (which the providential sign would supply), contrasting Judas's treachery.

themes: where wilt thou that we prepare

schools.summary: Ellicott: the signal common to Mark and Luke; the man with a pitcher (conspicuous, water-carrying women's work) an exact sign of Christ's foreknowledge (as the colt at the entry); the secrecy guarding the place from Judas so he could not betray before the feast; the disciples to follow a stranger on Christ's word.

themes: a man bearing a pitcher of water
the place hidden from the traitor

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'the Master' = the Teacher/Rabbi the householder acknowledged (a disciple); Robertson on 'goodman of the house' (oikodespotēs); Christ claims the guest-chamber with quiet authority (as he claimed the colt) — the householder ready to receive him, God's provision prepared beforehand, all at the Master's disposal.

themes: a disciple ready to receive him

schools.summary: Gill: furnished with all things necessary for the feast; Robertson on the 'large upper room' (Mark 14:15); the providence preparing a fit place — large, retired, furnished — for the last Passover and the institution of the Supper; the disciples still to 'make ready' (providence provides, servants do the work); the heart to be a fit room for Christ.

themes: the chamber for the great feast

schools.summary: Robertson: 'he had said' (past perfect) marking the precise correspondence; the disciples found all exactly as Christ foretold — a proof of his divine foreknowledge; their obedient faith (acting on a sign they could not arrange) rewarded with the finding and the privilege of preparing the feast — Christ's word never failing.

Bede: 'the hour' = the 14th day toward evening; Theophylact: Christ 'sat down' (reclined) for the meal after eating the lamb standing (Exodus 12:11) per the custom; Christ gathered with the twelve at the appointed hour for his last supper — a moment of solemn tenderness, the betrayer among the chosen at the table.

themes: he sat down with the twelve

schools.summary: Barnes: 'with desire I have desired' a Hebrew idiom for 'I have greatly desired' — Christ valuing the last fellowship, wishing to prepare them and partake once more; Theophylact: his 'last supper,' most precious; Cyril: he hid the place from Judas to keep the feast before being betrayed — the tender love of Christ, loving his own to the end (John 13:1).

themes: to eat this passover before I suffer

schools.summary: Gill: the Passover, with the ceremonial law, now abolished; Clarke: 'fulfilled' in Christ's death, the true Passover establishing the kingdom; Ellicott: fulfilled both in the cross and in every commemoration (the Supper, a communion with Christ) — the old feast giving way to the new, the type to the antitype, perfected in the kingdom.

themes: I will not any more eat thereof
fulfilled in the kingdom of God
type fulfilled in the cross and the Supper

schools.summary: Ellicott: Luke's distinctive order places a cup before the bread (and the covenant cup after) — most take this first as a Passover cup (the feast had several), distinct from the eucharistic cup; Clarke: shared hand to hand, not given to each; Christ gives thanks over the cup — thanksgiving and fellowship in the feast.

debates.question: Why does Luke record a cup before the bread?

sides.position: This first cup is a Passover cup (the feast had several), distinct from the covenant cup of v.20

themes: he took the cup and gave thanks

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'of the product (vine),' the words preceding the institution (parallel to v.16 on the Passover), Luke's order favoured; Clarke: 'until the kingdom come' = the near establishment of the gospel kingdom (the Spirit, the gospel, the Supper replacing the lamb), or the final consummation (Matthew 26:29) — a farewell that looks forward to renewed fellowship in the kingdom.

themes: I will not drink of the fruit of the vine
until the kingdom of God shall come
the forward look to the kingdom

schools.summary: Bede: the new feast substituting the Sacrament of Christ's body and blood for the lamb; Gregory of Nyssa/Cyril press a real presence — the consecrated bread is and is called the body of Christ ('this is my body,' received in faith).

The Reformed read 'this is my body' figuratively (= represents), grounding the Supper in 'this do in remembrance of me' — a memorial of Christ's one sacrifice, a showing-forth of his death, a spiritual communion by faith, not a corporal eating or repeated offering.

debates.question: Is the bread the body of Christ really, or a memorial sign?

sides.position: A real presence — the consecrated bread is and is called Christ's body
A figure/memorial — 'is' = represents; the Supper a remembrance and spiritual communion

themes: this is my body which is given for you
this do in remembrance of me

schools.summary: Clarke: the cup signifies the blood of the new covenant, ratified by Christ's blood shed on the cross; Ellicott/Gill: the cup 'after supper' the covenant cup, distinct from the Passover cup; the new covenant founded on Christ's blood (as the old by sacrifice, Exodus 24:8; Hebrews 9), shed for the remission of sins — the same real-presence/memorial debate as the bread.

themes: this cup is the new testament in my blood
the cup of the new covenant

schools.summary: Augustine: Christ speaks of the betrayer after the cup; Theophylact: the baseness of Judas who blushed not to be his guest, and Christ's example to labour for sinners to the end; Bede: he does not name him but charges the whole twelve, that the guilty might repent — Christ patiently seeking even the betrayer's salvation; the warning that sharing holy things is no security.

themes: the hand of him that betrayeth me

schools.summary: Robertson: 'as it hath been determined' (Acts 2:23), yet the 'woe' makes plain Judas's guilt is not absolved; Ellicott: 'determined' characteristic of Luke; the two truths joined — God's foreordination and man's responsible wickedness (the pattern of the cross, Acts 2:23) — God overruling sin yet the sinner justly condemned, no decree excusing sin.

debates.question: Does the foreordination of Christ's death excuse Judas?

sides.position: No — the 'woe' declares his full guilt; God's determination and man's guilt stand together

themes: the Son of man goeth as determined
God's decree no excuse for sin

schools.summary: Gill: they now inquired (vs earlier dumb surprise); Robertson: 'they all had their hands on the table — whose hand was it?'; the disciples (each asking 'Lord, is it I?') turning the question inward in humble self-suspicion, not presuming on their fidelity — the godly response to warned sin, fearing for oneself, contrasting Judas's brazen hypocrisy.

themes: which of them should do this

schools.summary: Theophylact: the strife grew from the inquiry about the betrayer (self-assertion); the Greek Expositor: who should be head with Christ departing; Ambrose: recorded not as excuse but as a warning against contention for precedence — pride persisting even at the Last Supper (cf. Luke 9:46), a rebuke to all ambition in the Church, answered by Christ's humility.

Barnes: the Gentile kings domineer and are flatteringly styled 'benefactors' (a royal surname, e.g. the Ptolemies 'Euergetes'); Christ does not condemn civil authority but sets the worldly pattern (lordship, love of honour) as the foil to his kingdom — the disciples' strife the worldly spirit, which Christ exposes to disown it.

themes: the kings of the Gentiles exercise lordship

schools.summary: Lightfoot reads 'as the younger' of age (Peter likely the eldest, hence his precedence) — even the foremost to behave as the least; Christ forbids the worldly pattern ('ye shall not be so') and reverses it — the greatest as the younger, the chief as servant; greatness in the kingdom is lowly service (Luke 9:48; Mark 10:43-44), not rank, a rebuke to the disciples' strife.

themes: the chief as he that serveth

schools.summary: Barnes: Christ cites his self-emptying service (John 13:13-14; Philippians 2:7); Ellicott: the undesigned coincidence — that very evening Christ had washed their feet, girded like a slave (John 13); Robertson: citing his own conduct to stop their contention; the Lord, greatest of all, among them as the servant — enforcing his teaching by his own example.

themes: whether is greater, he that sitteth or he that serveth
I am among you as he that serveth

schools.summary: Theophylact: woe to the traitor, blessing to the faithful who continued; Bede: not the first effort but long perseverance is rewarded, perseverance the prop of all virtues; Christ graciously owns the disciples' faithful continuance through his trials (imperfect, soon broken, yet honoured) — marking the difference between the betrayer who fell and those who stood by him.

Gill: a spiritual kingdom — the apostles' place in the church's government, and the kingdom of grace and glory shared with all saints (not temporal); Ambrose: the saints made in God's likeness (not equality), Christ his full image; Christ rewards perseverance with a kingdom modelled on the Father's gift to him — a greatness far surpassing the worldly precedence they strove for.

themes: I appoint unto you a kingdom
as my Father appointed unto me

schools.summary: Gill: the table of the kingdom — the Lord's table now, the marriage supper of the Lamb, the eternal feast where the saints hunger no more; Barnes: 'thrones judging' the apostles' future dignity and the saints' share in judgment (Matthew 19:28; 1 Corinthians 6:2); the kingdom's privileges (fellowship and rule) far surpassing the worldly precedence the disciples strove for.

themes: eat and drink at my table in my kingdom
sit on thrones judging the twelve tribes

schools.summary: Bede: lest the disciples boast in their own strength, Christ shows that left unprotected they would fall like the rest; Satan 'sifts as wheat' (the winnower shaking to scatter), but no faith is tried unless God permits; Theophylact: aimed at the bold Peter, lest he be proud — the believer's security in God's bounding the tempter, not his own strength.

debates.question: Why does Christ warn of Satan's sifting just after promising a kingdom?

sides.position: To humble the disciples lest they impute their standing to their own strength — they stand only by his protection

themes: Satan hath desired to sift you as wheat

schools.summary: Gill: Christ prayed particularly for Peter, in greatest danger; Lightfoot: Peter's courage failed utterly (the denial), but his faith, by Christ's prayer, was not finally shipwrecked (unlike Judas, unprayed-for); the perseverance of the believer rests on Christ's intercession (Hebrews 7:25), not his own strength — and the restored sinner is to strengthen the brethren.

Ellicott/Robertson detect a tone of indignation as well as devotion — Peter half-resented that a special prayer was needed, answering with a loud boast; the self-confidence presuming on his own strength, going before a fall (1 Corinthians 10:12); meeting Christ's warning with self-assurance rather than humble dependence, soon exposed by the denial.

themes: Lord, I am ready to go with thee

schools.summary: Clarke harmonizes 'this day' (Luke) with 'this night' (Matthew/Mark) — the Jewish day beginning at evening; Robertson: 'thrice' in all four Gospels, Peter even denying knowing Jesus; the precise foreknowledge (extent, depth, time) answering Peter's boast — humbling his self-confidence, and foretelling the fall to prepare his recovery (Luke 22:61-62).

themes: the cock shall not crow this day
thrice deny that thou knowest me

schools.summary: Cyril: Christ recalls the first mission (sent without provision, lacking nothing) to announce the coming struggle; Chrysostom's image of the swimming-teacher — Christ, present and supplying richly at first, withdraws his visible grace for a season that they may show their own strength (not abandoning, but strengthening their faith) — preparing them for the harder days of persecution.

themes: when I sent you without purse

schools.summary: Barnes: 'but now' the times are changed — the disciples now among strangers and enemies, to make provision; Lightfoot: the 'sword' not literal (cf. the rebuke of Peter's sword, Matthew 26:52) but a common proverbial figure warning vividly of the near danger and hostility, to suppress their rivalries by the pressing common peril — the harder conditions of discipleship in a hostile world.

debates.question: Is the 'sword' a literal call to arm?

sides.position: No — a proverbial figure of the coming danger and hostility, not a call to violence (cf. Matthew 26:52)

themes: but now, he that hath a purse
let him sell his garment and buy a sword
the sword a figure of danger

schools.summary: Barnes: 'reckoned among the transgressors' (Isaiah 53:12) — not as a transgressor but treated as one, crucified with thieves, though sinless (Hebrews 7:26); Lightfoot: 'the things concerning me have an end' — his earthly work closing; the divine 'must,' the prophecy fulfilled, the innocent numbered with the guilty in his substitution.

themes: his course coming to an end

schools.summary: Gill: the disciples took the 'sword' literally, producing two (brought against Galilean robbers, one Peter's); Barnes on the Galileans going armed; 'It is enough' best read not as approval (two swords no armoury, soon rebuked) but as closing the misunderstood conversation — the disciples' dullness to figurative speech, Christ's patience, prelude to the rebuke of the sword.

sides.position: Not approval of arms, but a closing of the misunderstood conversation ('enough of this')

themes: prelude to the rebuke of the sword

schools.summary: Bede: Christ goes to his wonted place of retirement, where the betrayer could most easily find him — not fleeing but offering himself; the voluntariness of his Passion, going willingly to meet the arrest he foreknew (John 10:18), laying down his life of himself; Olivet, his place of prayer, now the scene of his agony.

Robertson: 'keep on praying not to enter' (be overcome by) temptation — real temptation, not mere trial; Christ knew its power and the need of prayer, illuminating the Lord's Prayer (Matthew 6:13); prayer the safeguard against falling in trial — the disciples, sleeping instead of praying, soon overcome (fleeing, denying).

themes: pray that ye enter not into temptation

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'about a stone's cast' an eyewitness touch peculiar to Luke, the tense ('was praying') implying sustained prayer; Robertson harmonizes the postures (kneeled/fell on the ground/on his face) as true at different moments; Christ's earnest, solitary prayer in his agony, withdrawing apart to pour out his soul before the Father.

Gill: 'if thou be willing' = if consistent with thy will of saving sinners; Ellicott: an echo of the Lord's Prayer ('Thy will be done'), the petition subject to the Father's will; Robertson on 'be done' (keep on being done); Christ's sinless human shrinking from the cup, yet wholly submitting — the pattern of submissive prayer, the costly yielding that embraced the cross.

themes: Father, if thou be willing, remove this cup
not my will but thine be done

schools.summary: Bede: the angel strengthening Christ a testimony to both natures — as God he needed no creature, but as man (sorrowful for our sakes) he was comforted; Theophylact: the power of prayer answered in adversity; Ambrose guards against reading the agony as inherent weakness — borne for us; vv.43-44 absent from some MSS but widely attested.

debates.question: Does the angel strengthening Christ deny his deity?

sides.position: No — a testimony to his two natures; as God he needed no help, but as man he was truly comforted, for our sakes

schools.summary: Gill: the 'agony' a conflict (with Satan assaulting him, John 14:30) and above all the prospect of bearing sin and the wrath due to it (not death merely but death as sin's punishment); Clarke notes (without endorsing) the 'divine wrath' view; Chrysostom: the bloody sweat proves the real incarnation and real suffering — the costly love that endured such agony for us.

debates.question: What was the cause of Christ's agony and bloody sweat?

sides.position: The prospect of bearing sin and the wrath due to it (death as sin's punishment)
A conflict with Satan assaulting him in the garden

themes: sweat as great drops of blood
the bearing of sin and wrath

schools.summary: Ellicott: Luke alone adds 'for sorrow,' tracing the sleep to its cause — prolonged grief's numbing effect (cf. 'believing not for joy,' Luke 24:41), the physician's psychological insight; Robertson: Luke omits the painful details of Christ's three turnings; the disciples' love real but flesh weak, overcome by sorrow when they should have watched and prayed.

Ellicott: Luke briefer, omitting the threefold warning and the half-permissive 'sleep on now'; Robertson: the reproach without the bitter details; Christ rouses the disciples ('Why sleep ye?') and renews the charge of v.40 to pray against temptation — the persistence of his care even in his agony, the urgency of prayer with the trial now at hand.

Cyril: 'one of the twelve' signifies the enormity — the honoured apostle became the cause of Christ's murder, his name held in abhorrence; Chrysostom: the soul once captive to sin, like an incurable wound, yields to no admonition — Judas unmoved by Christ's every warning; the kiss of love made the instrument of betrayal, the deepest hypocrisy.

Gill: Christ names him 'Judas' to show he knows him, recalling all he was (apostle, friend, trusted); Barnes: every word emphatic — 'betrayest thou the Son of man with a kiss?' exposing the treachery, ingratitude, and hypocrisy; yet a final tender appeal to the conscience, the kiss (the token of love) made the badge of betrayal.

themes: Judas, betrayest thou the Son of man
the final appeal to the betrayer

schools.summary: Ellicott: probably the three nearest; Robertson: Luke alone gives the question, the disciples having the two swords (v.38), Peter not waiting for the answer; the carnal impulse to defend Christ by force, a misunderstanding of his kingdom (John 18:36) — his way is submission, not the sword (Matthew 26:52).

themes: shall we smite with the sword
not the way of his kingdom

schools.summary: Gill: Peter (named by John 18:10) struck Malchus, not waiting for Christ's answer; Robertson: Luke the Physician alone notes the 'right' ear (followed by John) — the eyewitness precision; Peter's impetuous, misguided zeal (his boast flaring in violence), the carnal method contrary to Christ's will, the prelude to the healing miracle.

themes: one of them smote the servant

schools.summary: Ellicott: the incident peculiar to Luke; Clarke: 'suffer ye thus far' a command to the disciples (cease resistance) or a plea to reach and heal Malchus; Christ healed the ear his disciple cut off — mercy to an enemy in the moment of arrest (Ambrose: he suffered not even his enemies to be wounded), his last miracle, a healing, rebuking the sword.

debates.question: To whom is 'Suffer ye thus far' addressed?

sides.position: To the disciples — cease resistance, let things proceed
To the captors — a plea for leave to heal Malchus

themes: he touched his ear and healed him
the last miracle before the Passion

schools.summary: Ellicott: Luke alone records the presence of the chief priests, captains, and elders in person; Robertson: 'as against a robber' (treating Jesus like Barabbas); Christ exposes the injustice — armed, by night, with swords and staves against the innocent, unresisting Teacher; the show of force betraying the injustice and cowardice of the arrest.

themes: be ye come out as against a thief
the open Teacher seized like a felon

schools.summary: Gill: Christ taught daily in the temple, yet they took him not — exposing their cowardice (resorting to a stealthy night arrest); Lightfoot: 'this is your hour, and the power of darkness' — the serpent in Judas, the devil's chains for a moment loosed, the foreordained hour permitted to evil; yet a bounded 'hour,' overruled by God for redemption, to be overthrown by the resurrection.

themes: daily with you in the temple
this is your hour, and the power of darkness

schools.summary: Ambrose: Christ 'holden' not in his divine nature nor through weakness, but according to his bodily nature — willing captivity, not compromised deity; Augustine harmonizes (Annas then Caiaphas); Chrysostom commends Peter's courage in following (when others fled) yet 'afar off' betrays fear — the perilous middle course, the seed of his denial.

themes: brought him into the high priest's house
willing captivity and the seed of denial

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'court-yard' not 'hall' (an open space); Robertson: the servants kindled a blaze (cool April night), all seated round, Peter among them; the imprudence of Peter placing himself among Christ's enemies in the firelight — in the way of temptation, seeking the world's warmth, the prelude to his denial (cf. Luke 22:40).

themes: kindled a fire in the hall

schools.summary: Clarke/Ellicott: the maid 'earnestly looked' (Luke's word for a fixed gaze, cf. 4:20) by the firelight, recognizing Peter; the 'also' explained by John (the other disciple known); the fire Peter sought for warmth betrayed him, the least accuser (a servant-girl) beginning his fall — temptation in a small, unexpected form overthrowing the unprepared.

themes: this man was also with him

schools.summary: Gill: startled at the maid, Peter 'roundly and at once' denied — he who boasted he would die with Christ frighted by a servant-girl at the first attack; Robertson: 'I know him not' exactly as Jesus predicted (Luke 22:34); the swift, shameful fall the fruit of self-confidence (1 Corinthians 10:12), the boast collapsing at the slightest temptation.

Ellicott: 'Man' implies anger/impatience (cf. Luke 12:14); Robertson/Wesley: the harmony of the accounts — several joined in the accusation (here a man, elsewhere a maid), supplementing not contradicting; Peter's second, blunter denial deepening the fall — sin breeding sin, the soul once denying carried further, each step harder to retrace.

debates.question: How are the varying accusers in the four Gospels reconciled?

sides.position: Several persons joined in charging Peter (maid and man), the accounts supplementing one another

schools.summary: Robertson: 'confidently affirmed' (kept affirming strongly), 'for he is a Galilean' — his accent betraying him (Matthew 26:73); Ellicott: the hour's interval and the strong affirmation peculiar to Luke; the third, most confident and public charge, the net closing — Peter's own speech betraying the Lord he denied with it, the climax of the fall at hand.

Gill: Peter's third denial reinforced with cursing and swearing (Matthew/Mark) — the deepest fall, profanely abjuring his Lord; Robertson: each denial tangles him more, the cock crowing the very moment he spoke; the appointed sign (Luke 22:34) fulfilled to the moment, the means (with the Lord's look) of recalling Peter to repentance — mercy in the depth of the fall.

themes: Man, I know not what thou sayest

schools.summary: Ellicott: the Lord's look (the turning-point of Peter's life) recorded only by Luke, as Christ was led from Annas to the trial; Clarke: 'this delicate reproof' — not words but a look, of grieved love not anger; it recalled the forgotten word (Luke 22:34) and broke Peter's heart — the gracious dealing of Christ recalling the fallen to repentance, unlike despairing Judas.

themes: the Lord turned and looked upon Peter

schools.summary: Robertson: 'he burst into tears' (ingressive aorist), bitter the common expression for deep grief; Barnes: the godly sorrow that works repentance (2 Corinthians 7:10; Psalm 130) — Peter's bitter weeping the pattern of true repentance, contrasting despairing Judas; the fruit of Christ's intercession (Luke 22:32), his fall issuing in repentance and recovery, not despair.

themes: Peter went out and wept bitterly

schools.summary: Augustine harmonizes the order (Luke puts the denial before the mocking, Matthew/Mark reverse — not contradiction but arrangement); Chrysostom: the Lord of heaven and earth suffers the mockings of the ungodly, an example of patience; Theophylact: the Lord of prophets derided as a false prophet — Christ's meek endurance under abuse, returning not evil for evil.

themes: the men that held Jesus mocked him

schools.summary: Robertson: 'blindfolded' (a veil around); Ellicott: the taunt rests on the popular view identifying prophecy with clairvoyance; Wesley: the abuse repeated before and after condemnation; the mockers deride the source of prophecy as a false prophet, their mockery exposing their own blindness (the blindfolded Christ saw all) — Christ enduring in silence.

themes: prophesy, who is it that smote thee
the mock of his prophetic office

schools.summary: Gill: the mockers denied his deity and sonship, mocked his offices, called him a mere wicked man.

Wesley's reversal: they charged Christ with blasphemy for claiming to be the Son of God, but the evangelist fixes the blasphemy on them, because he really was so — the word 'blasphemously' itself a confession of Christ's deity, for reviling a mere man would not be blasphemy; their abuse the true blasphemy against the Lord of glory.

debates.question: Why does the evangelist call the mockers' words 'blasphemy'?

sides.position: Because Christ really was the Son of God — so reviling him was true blasphemy, fixed on them

themes: many other things blasphemously spake they

schools.summary: Robertson: 'the council' = the Sanhedrin (presbyterion), composed of elders, scribes, and chief priests; the formal daybreak session convened to give the condemnation the colour of law — not a mob but the supreme court of Israel, the nation's leaders officially rejecting and condemning their Messiah, fulfilling the prophecies of the rejected Christ.

themes: as soon as it was day

schools.summary: Ellicott: Luke omits the false witnesses and temple-charge, giving the direct question; 'If I tell you, ye will not believe' (only in Luke), interpreted by John (8:58; 10:30) — Christ had told them and they believed not; Robertson: the condition assumes their unbelief; the futility of testimony to the determined unbeliever, unbelief a matter of the hardened will, not want of evidence.

themes: if I tell you, ye will not believe
the futility of testimony to unbelief

schools.summary: Ellicott: the words refer to Christ's recent question (Matthew 22:41-46, the Christ as David's son or Lord, which they could not answer) — an indirect protest against their claim to judge him; Clarke: he knew they were determined to kill him; Christ exposes the council's bad faith — they would neither answer his questions nor release him, their verdict fixed, no impartial tribunal.

themes: if I also ask you, ye will not answer

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'from this time forth shall the Son of man be sitting' (Luke's form) — the exaltation beginning now, the kingdom established through his death and resurrection; Clarke: 'hereafter' = from this very time; Barnes: the session at God's right hand (Hebrews 1:3; Psalm 110:1; Daniel 7:13-14) — the condemned prisoner declaring his enthronement, the prisoner become the Judge.

themes: the Son of man sit on the right hand of God

schools.summary: Ellicott: the question asked by the whole company (only in Luke); 'Ye say that I am' a complete affirmation (equivalent to the plainer forms in Matthew/Mark), the 'I am' echoing the divine name (John 8:58); Clarke: they all insisted on the answer; Christ openly confesses his divine Sonship, sealing his condemnation by the truth — the faithful Witness against the fearful Peter.

themes: art thou then the Son of God
sealing his condemnation by the truth

schools.summary: Clarke: the council, having Christ's profession of his divine Sonship, judged it blasphemy and resolved to condemn him to death, then bring him to Pilate (the Jews lacking power to execute); the council condemned Christ not on any proven crime but on his true confession — counting his deity blasphemy, the perversity of unbelief, the transition to the cross.

themes: what need we any further witness
